caveats: a: OEWS combines computers, storage, and peripherals.
a: The product mix spans commodity consumer devices and complex enterprise terminals with very different labor content.
rho: Revenue and product-quality improvements are not all labor substitution.
rho: Low-volume specialized terminals and mass-market peripherals have different data scale and integration economics.
e: The 25.32% Computers/Peripherals margin bridge may overstate EBITDA for hardware assembly and channel-heavy firms.
e: Industry classification can blur true manufacture, integration, import, and resale.
s5: The source is not industry-specific and is not a completed-transfer rate.
s5: Product-line acquisitions and brand licenses may not transfer an eligible operating firm.
q: HP and Corsair are larger and have different channels and purchasing power than LMM firms.
q: Printers, POS terminals, industrial scanners, monitors, and gaming devices have different pricing power.
d5: BLS 334100 includes computers and storage and measures real value rather than units.
d5: Recent company results are short-period examples, not an industry forecast.
o: Imports can displace domestic operators without eliminating operator-required physical demand.
o: Some terminals become software on general-purpose devices, which is reflected partly in d5.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat product and configured-system sales to enterprises, retailers, hospitality operators, financial institutions, governments, distributors, integrators, gamers, creators, and consumers, priced per device or configuration with channel discounts, warranty, support, consumables, software, and service terms.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive plants, storage-device makers, communications equipment, pure importers and resellers, repair-only businesses, software-only vendors, component makers, passive entities, and firms without transferable operations.
lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. firms repeatedly designing, assembling, manufacturing, testing, and selling computer terminals and non-storage peripherals such as printers, monitors, scanners, input devices, point-of-sale terminals, and specialized interface equipment to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures broader 334100 and shows management, computer, engineering, sales, office, production, maintenance, and logistics roles. NIST documents AI uses in quality, maintenance, forecasting, scheduling, and resource management. I translate those occupations and uses into wage-weighted remaining exposure for peripherals; the industry's mix of digital product development and commercial work raises exposure, while outsourced and automated assembly lowers incremental factory opportunity.
caveats: OEWS combines computers, storage, and peripherals.
The product mix spans commodity consumer devices and complex enterprise terminals with very different labor content.
a.change_evidence: A task-and-wage study across printer, POS, scanner, display, input-device, gaming, and specialty peripheral makers that distinguishes existing automation and outsourcing would materially change the range.
a.rationale: AI can assist product and firmware engineering, configuration, documentation, visual inspection, test analysis, failure diagnosis, demand and inventory planning, sourcing, quoting, sales content, support, and administration. Physical assembly, calibration, rework, certification, handling, and accountable release remain.

d5.bridge: BLS projects broader 334100 real output from $43.5 billion in 2024 to $51.0 billion in 2034, a 1.6% annual rate. HP reports declining printer units and revenue, while PAR reports 2025 growth in peripherals, terminals, kiosks, and display hardware and Corsair reports mixed but growing creator and gaming categories. I translate the broader projection and divergent segment evidence into a five-year constant-quality demand ratio. Broader computer and storage growth likely overstates traditional peripheral demand.
caveats: BLS 334100 includes computers and storage and measures real value rather than units.
Recent company results are short-period examples, not an industry forecast.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year U.S. shipment forecast by printer, display, POS, kiosk, scanner, input, gaming, and industrial-terminal category, reconciled to domestic production, would materially improve the estimate.
d5.rationale: POS modernization, self-service, automation, gaming, creators, hybrid work, security, and specialized industrial interfaces support demand. Mobile substitution, paperless workflows, device integration, longer replacement cycles, imports, and commoditization create downside.

caveats: The 25.32% Computers/Peripherals margin bridge may overstate EBITDA for hardware assembly and channel-heavy firms.
Industry classification can blur true manufacture, integration, import, and resale.
e.change_evidence: A named review of the 105 candidates covering ultimate ownership, domestic manufacturing substance, external recurring revenue, normalized EBITDA, channel and customer concentration, certifications, IP, and transferability would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: The frozen estimate of 105 firms plausibly includes many independent makers of POS, kiosks, scanners, specialized printers, industrial terminals, gaming devices, and niche peripherals. Large OEM divisions, captive units, import-led brands, contract-assembly-light businesses, thin margins, customer concentration, and businesses outside the EBITDA band reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 334118 ($4,571,558,721) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 334118 ($4,798,735,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2532 (margins.json[334]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Computers/Peripherals EBITDA/Sales 25.32%); revenue/employee $331,336 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 334118); class <5: 84 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,021,714 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $258,698 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 45 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,461,089 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $623,148 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 45 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,027,844 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,273,050 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 60 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,324,200 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,373,687 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 26 firms, avg revenue/firm $45,998,462 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $11,646,810 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 34 firms, avg revenue/firm $70,014,618 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,727,701 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 105

caveats: Imports can displace domestic operators without eliminating operator-required physical demand.
Some terminals become software on general-purpose devices, which is reflected partly in d5.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of broad software or mobile substitution eliminating peripheral categories, customer self-assembly, or autonomous contract production removing the lens operator would lower the estimate.
o.rationale: Remaining demand requires an accountable maker to design or integrate, test, certify, secure, warrant, and deliver physical interface devices. Software, mobile devices, and integrated systems can eliminate some categories, but cannot supply the remaining physical peripherals.

q.bridge: HP reports highly competitive printer hardware markets, 2025 printer unit volume down 4.2%, hardware average selling prices down 1.5%, and pricing pressure across customer segments. Corsair reports both price increases and offsetting promotions and tariffs in gaming peripherals. I translate these large-company disclosures to LMM peripheral makers: commodity and channel products pass gains through quickly, while specialized terminals, qualification, software integration, service, and proprietary ergonomics can retain more.
caveats: HP and Corsair are larger and have different channels and purchasing power than LMM firms.
Printers, POS terminals, industrial scanners, monitors, and gaming devices have different pricing power.
q.change_evidence: Eligible-firm contracts, channel rebates, promotion and price histories, component pass-through, attach revenue, renewal data, and gross-margin persistence after automation would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Visible components, online comparison, distributor leverage, promotions, and short product cycles compress margins. Specialized workflow fit, certifications, installed-base integration, reliability, consumables, software, service, and rapid customization protect a minority of gains.

caveats: Revenue and product-quality improvements are not all labor substitution.
Low-volume specialized terminals and mass-market peripherals have different data scale and integration economics.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments reporting engineering cycle time, first-pass yield, test escapes, returns, forecast error, inventory, support resolution, overrides, and labor hours saved would narrow the estimate.
rho.rationale: Digital design files, firmware, test logs, support records, and channel data favor implementation. Short product cycles, fragmented product lines, cybersecurity, privacy, certification, supplier variability, legacy ERP, model errors, and LMM change capacity constrain deployment.

s5.bridge: SBA reports that 47% of family-business owners expecting retirement within five years lack a successor. I treat it as broad succession pressure, then discount it because it is cross-industry and because peripheral brands may close, license IP, sell a product line, or accept minority capital without a qualifying control transfer.
caveats: The source is not industry-specific and is not a completed-transfer rate.
Product-line acquisitions and brand licenses may not transfer an eligible operating firm.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, and sector M&A records matched to eligible 334118 firms, plus owner-age and succession interviews, would replace the proxy.
s5.rationale: Founder-owned specialist device makers plausibly face succession or strategic-sale pressure, while fast obsolescence, channel dependence, certifications, inventory, and key-person engineering can impede a transfer.

principal_driver: A broad niche-product base combines transferable specialist firms with digitized engineering, test, support, and planning work.
principal_weakness: Commodity peripherals face rapid price competition and substitution, making demand and retained benefit highly segment-dependent.

caveats: S1's current Census response does not display the cited 334118 definition or examples. Replacing it with a stable Census manual or downloadable definition would repair traceability, but would not change the base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The labor-exposure primitive a is an honestly labeled proxy, but S2 and S3 establish only a broad occupational mix and generic manufacturing use cases; they do not reproduce the claimed wage-weighted 334118 exposure or its 0.21-0.47 range.

Eligibility e remains an unverified estimate layered on a margin-bridged 105-firm dataset anchor. The record discloses the likely upward-bias channels, so this weakness supports caveated publication rather than rejection.

The s5 bridge converts a secondary, cross-industry family-business succession statistic into a five-year qualifying-transfer estimate; it is not evidence of completed control transfers in peripheral manufacturing.

The q evidence comes from large public companies with purchasing power, channel mix, and product breadth unlike LMM specialist makers, so the retained-benefit range remains highly judgmental despite the supported disclosures.
The d5 bridge combines a broader 334100 ten-year real-output projection with selected one-year company results; it does not constitute a five-year U.S. shipment forecast for the lens categories.
The o range of 0.94-0.998 is a narrow, high judgment range not measured by S1, S4, or S6; its interpretation is plausible only because o is conditional on demand already surviving in d5.

The packet marks the lens heterogeneous=false while repeatedly describing extreme heterogeneity across printers, POS, scanners, displays, gaming devices, and specialized terminals. This metadata inconsistency is disclosed in substance and does not change the base tier or action.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census query URL reaches the official NAICS page, but its current response renders a blank definition result and does not expose the claimed 334118 title, definition, or examples. This is a traceability defect rather than a material economic defect.

The May 2023 OEWS page is explicitly for broader NAICS 334100 and reports management, computer, engineering, sales, office, maintenance, production, and material-moving occupations and wages, matching the limited claim and disclosed population caveat.

NIST describes manufacturing AI uses in predictive maintenance, quality control, demand forecasting, resource management, production scheduling, document management, and digital twins, and lists data, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy-integration barriers.

HP's fiscal 2025 10-K describes highly competitive printer-hardware pricing, a 3.7% Printing revenue decline, printer unit volume down 4.2%, and hardware ASP down 1.5%, supporting the cited observations.

Corsair's fiscal 2025 10-K reports growth in creator products alongside lower North American gaming-peripheral demand, and attributes peripheral-segment margin changes to price increases offset by greater promotion and tariff costs.

PAR's fiscal 2025 10-K reports hardware revenue up 22.3%, with increases from scanners, printers, components, terminals, kiosks, and kitchen display systems, exactly supporting the company-example claim.

The SBA blog states that 47% of owners expecting retirement within five years lack a successor. The record correctly treats this secondary, cross-industry family-business statistic only as a broad proxy.
The BLS industry table reports broader NAICS 334100 real output of $43.5 billion in 2024 and $51.0 billion in 2034, with 1.6% annual growth, matching the cited values and disclosed scope.
